relations: The immune system is part of the human body, working to fight off infections and restore health
The skin acts as one of the first lines of defense for the immune system
Mucous membranes are part of the immune system's physical barriers against pathogens
The stomach's acidic environment contributes to the immune system's defense mechanisms
White blood cells are crucial components of the immune system's response to infections
The immune system fights against bacteria to prevent and eliminate infections
The immune system targets viruses as part of its defense mechanisms
Fungi are combated by the immune system to protect the body from infections
The immune system works to rid the body of parasites to maintain health
Both neutrophils and macrophages are involved in the initial response to infections by engulfing and destroying pathogens
Dendritic cells present antigens to T cells, a type of lymphocyte, to initiate a specific immune response
Both natural killer cells and lymphocytes (T cells) are involved in directly attacking infected cells
Lymphocytes differentiate into memory cells, which are crucial for the development of immunological memory
Complement proteins and cytokines both enhance and regulate the immune response against pathogens
Immunological memory is the principle behind vaccination, allowing for a faster and more effective immune response upon re-exposure to pathogens
Autoimmune diseases can lead to chronic inflammation, contributing to tissue damage and dysfunction
Chronic inflammation is a characteristic of rheumatoid arthritis, leading to joint damage
Chronic inflammation is a key feature of lupus, affecting various organs
Chronic inflammation contributes to the neurological damage seen in multiple sclerosis
Chronic inflammation can contribute to the development of heart disease by damaging arteries and other heart tissues
Chronic inflammation is known to play a role in the development of diabetes by affecting insulin resistance
Chronic inflammation can lead to cancer by promoting abnormal cell growth and mutations
